sections.title: 🧒 The Story
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
Before Lambda, even tiny background tasks needed a server:
→ resize uploaded image? spin up EC2
→ run nightly cleanup? keep cron server alive 24/7
→ process webhook? maintain autoscaling group for occasional traffic

That meant:
→ patching OS for code that ran 300ms
→ paying for idle compute
→ guessing capacity for spiky workloads

Lambda changed the model:
event happens → AWS runs your code → AWS stops billing when code stops.

MENTAL MODEL:
Lambda is not "a tiny server."
Lambda is an event execution engine.
You give AWS:
→ code
→ memory/timeout
→ trigger

AWS gives you:
→ automatic scaling
→ no server management
→ pay-per-use

Best for:
→ event-driven workloads
→ bursty traffic
→ glue code between services
→ APIs with variable load
```

---

→ process webhook? maintain autoscaling group for occasional traffic

That meant:

Lambda changed the model:

You give AWS:

AWS gives you:

sections.title: ⚙️ Lambda Internals — How it Actually Works
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
EXECUTION ENVIRONMENT:
When Lambda runs your function, it creates an isolated execution environment:
→ Micro-VM (firecracker — AWS's open-source VMM, starts in < 125ms)
→ Has: CPU (proportional to memory), RAM (you choose), /tmp disk (512MB-10GB)
→ Isolated: cannot see other Lambda executions
→ Runs your function handler
→ After handler returns: environment stays alive (frozen, not terminated)
→ Next invocation: environment REUSED if within ~15 minutes of last use

EXECUTION ENVIRONMENT LIFECYCLE:
INIT PHASE (cold start):
  → Download code package from S3 (< 50ms usually)
  → Start firecracker VM
  → Initialize runtime (Node.js, Python, Java JVM...)
  → Run your INITIALIZATION CODE (outside handler)
  → Ready to handle first invocation

INVOKE PHASE:
  → Run your handler function
  → Return response
  → Environment frozen (waiting for next invocation)

SHUTDOWN PHASE:
  → If no invocation for ~15 minutes → environment terminated
  → Next invocation: full cold start again
```

### Cold Starts — Deep Understanding

```
COLD START = the INIT phase happening before your code runs
Duration by language:
→ Python:   50–200ms
→ Node.js:  50–300ms
→ Java:     500ms–2s (JVM startup is slow)
→ Go:       50–150ms
→ .NET:     200–400ms
→ Java with SnapStart: < 100ms (AWS pre-initializes JVM snapshot)

WHAT CAUSES COLD STARTS:
→ Function not invoked recently (environment shut down)
→ Scaling: more concurrent invocations than warm environments
→ Deployment: new code version deployed (old environments die)
→ VPC Lambda: ENI creation adds 1-10 seconds (improved significantly in 2019)

REDUCING COLD STARTS:
1. Keep packages small → faster code download
   → Don't bundle unnecessary dependencies
   → Use Lambda Layers for large shared dependencies (pre-loaded)
   → Python: remove boto3 from zip (already in runtime)

2. Initialize expensive things OUTSIDE the handler:
   // GOOD — runs once per environment (initialization code)
   const dbClient = new DatabaseClient(process.env.DB_URL);
   const s3Client = new S3Client({ region: 'us-east-1' });
   
   // RUNS ON EVERY INVOCATION (handler)
   exports.handler = async (event) => {
     // dbClient and s3Client already initialized!
     const result = await dbClient.query(...);
     return result;
   };

3. Provisioned Concurrency:
   → Pre-initialize N execution environments
   → They're ALWAYS warm (no cold start for those N)
   → Cost: $0.000004646/GB-second (even when idle)
   → Use for: latency-sensitive APIs that can't afford cold starts
   
4. Lambda SnapStart (Java only):
   → AWS takes snapshot of initialized JVM
   → Restore from snapshot instead of full init
   → Cold start: 2000ms → < 100ms for Java

5. Schedule warm-up pings (cheap but imperfect):
   → EventBridge rule: every 5 minutes → invoke Lambda with "warm" event
   → Handler: if event.source === 'warmup' → return immediately
   → Keeps ONE environment warm (doesn't help during scaling)
   → Provisioned Concurrency is better but costs money
```

### Concurrency — How Lambda Scales

```
CONCURRENCY = number of function instances running simultaneously

CONCURRENT EXECUTIONS:
→ 100 requests at same time → 100 Lambda instances running simultaneously
→ Each instance is independent (separate execution environment)
→ Account default limit: 1,000 concurrent executions per region
→ Can request increase (no hard maximum for most regions)

SCALING BEHAVIOR:
→ Each new concurrent request that has no warm environment → cold start
→ Scaling is very fast (hundreds per minute for synchronous invocations)
→ SQS/Kinesis triggered Lambda: scales based on queue depth/shard count

RESERVED CONCURRENCY:
→ Guarantee X concurrent executions for THIS function
→ Other functions cannot "steal" that capacity
→ Also acts as throttle: if function tries to exceed X → throttled
→ Use for: critical functions that need guaranteed capacity
→ Use for: prevent one function from consuming all account concurrency

PROVISIONED CONCURRENCY:
→ Pre-initialize X environments (always warm)
→ Guaranteed to handle X concurrent requests with zero cold start
→ Cost: even when not handling requests
→ Use for: latency-critical APIs (checkout, payments, real-time)
→ Can combine with Auto Scaling for Provisioned Concurrency
   (scale provisioned concurrency up/down based on schedule or metrics)

THROTTLING:
→ Function hits concurrency limit → new invocations → throttled
→ Synchronous: returns 429 TooManyRequestsException → caller handles
→ Async (SQS/EventBridge): Lambda retries automatically (2 times for async)
→ SQS: messages stay in queue, Lambda retries when concurrency available
```

---

When Lambda runs your function, it creates an isolated execution environment:

sections.title: 🔌 Lambda Triggers (Event Sources)
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
SYNCHRONOUS INVOCATIONS (caller waits for response):
→ ALB: HTTP request → Lambda processes → returns HTTP response
→ API Gateway: same pattern
→ Lambda → Lambda: one function invokes another directly
→ Cognito: custom auth during user auth flow
→ CloudFront: Lambda@Edge

ASYNCHRONOUS INVOCATIONS (fire and forget):
→ S3: object created/deleted → Lambda notified (can fail silently)
→ SNS: message published → Lambda notified
→ EventBridge: event matched rule → Lambda invoked
→ CloudWatch Events/Logs: scheduled or log-based triggers
→ Async: Lambda retries 2 times on failure, then → DLQ if configured

EVENT SOURCE MAPPING (Lambda manages polling):
→ SQS: Lambda polls queue, gets batch → invokes with batch
→ Kinesis: Lambda polls stream shards → invokes with records
→ DynamoDB Streams: Lambda polls stream → invokes with changes
→ MSK/Kafka: Lambda polls → invokes with messages
→ Lambda manages the polling → you just handle the records

SQS EVENT SOURCE MAPPING SETTINGS:
Batch size: 1-10,000 messages per invocation
Batch window: accumulate messages for up to N seconds before invoking (max 300s)
Concurrency: Lambda scales based on number of messages in flight
On failure: send failed batches to DLQ or SNS
Partial batch response: report which messages in batch succeeded (others retry)
```

---

blocks.content:
SYNCHRONOUS INVOCATIONS (caller waits for response):
→ ALB: HTTP request → Lambda processes → returns HTTP response
→ API Gateway: same pattern
→ Lambda → Lambda: one function invokes another directly
→ Cognito: custom auth during user auth flow
→ CloudFront: Lambda@Edge

ASYNCHRONOUS INVOCATIONS (fire and forget):
→ S3: object created/deleted → Lambda notified (can fail silently)
→ SNS: message published → Lambda notified
→ EventBridge: event matched rule → Lambda invoked
→ CloudWatch Events/Logs: scheduled or log-based triggers
→ Async: Lambda retries 2 times on failure, then → DLQ if configured

EVENT SOURCE MAPPING (Lambda manages polling):
→ SQS: Lambda polls queue, gets batch → invokes with batch
→ Kinesis: Lambda polls stream shards → invokes with records
→ DynamoDB Streams: Lambda polls stream → invokes with changes
→ MSK/Kafka: Lambda polls → invokes with messages
→ Lambda manages the polling → you just handle the records

SQS EVENT SOURCE MAPPING SETTINGS:
Batch size: 1-10,000 messages per invocation
Batch window: accumulate messages for up to N seconds before invoking (max 300s)
Concurrency: Lambda scales based on number of messages in flight
On failure: send failed batches to DLQ or SNS
Partial batch response: report which messages in batch succeeded (others retry)

revisionPoints: → ALB: HTTP request → Lambda processes → returns HTTP response
→ API Gateway: same pattern
→ Lambda → Lambda: one function invokes another directly
→ Cognito: custom auth during user auth flow
→ CloudFront: Lambda@Edge
→ S3: object created/deleted → Lambda notified (can fail silently)
→ SNS: message published → Lambda notified
→ EventBridge: event matched rule → Lambda invoked

sections.title: 🌐 Lambda in VPC
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
BY DEFAULT: Lambda NOT in VPC
→ Cannot reach VPC resources (RDS, ElastiCache, private EC2)
→ CAN reach internet (API Gateway, S3, DynamoDB via public endpoints)

WITH VPC CONFIGURATION:
→ Lambda gets an ENI in your specified subnet
→ CAN reach VPC resources (same VPC)
→ CANNOT reach internet by default!
   Need NAT Gateway in private subnet for internet access

WHEN TO PUT LAMBDA IN VPC:
✅ Lambda needs to connect to RDS (private subnet)
✅ Lambda needs to connect to ElastiCache
✅ Lambda needs to access private services in VPC

WHEN NOT TO PUT LAMBDA IN VPC:
❌ Lambda only calls AWS services (S3, DynamoDB, SQS) — use VPC endpoints instead
❌ Lambda only calls public internet APIs — no VPC needed
→ VPC Lambda: slightly higher cold starts (ENI management)
→ Use VPC endpoints to keep AWS service calls private without VPC Lambda

RDS CONNECTION PROBLEM WITH LAMBDA:
Lambda scales to 1,000 concurrent → 1,000 connections to RDS → connection pool exhausted!
Each Lambda execution opens a new DB connection.

SOLUTION: RDS Proxy
→ RDS Proxy sits between Lambda and RDS
→ Pools and reuses connections
→ Lambda → RDS Proxy (multiplexes connections) → RDS
→ 1,000 concurrent Lambdas → RDS Proxy → 20 actual DB connections
→ RDS Proxy is in VPC (same subnet as Lambda)
→ Supports: IAM authentication, Secrets Manager rotation
```

---

BY DEFAULT: Lambda NOT in VPC
→ Cannot reach VPC resources (RDS, ElastiCache, private EC2)
→ CAN reach internet (API Gateway, S3, DynamoDB via public endpoints)

→ Use VPC endpoints to keep AWS service calls private without VPC Lambda

✅ Lambda needs to access private services in VPC

sections.title: 💡 Lambda Best Practices
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
CODE:
✅ Handler should be small and focused (single responsibility)
✅ Initialize SDK clients and DB connections OUTSIDE handler
✅ Use environment variables for config (not hardcoded)
✅ Retrieve secrets from Secrets Manager (cache in memory between invocations)
✅ Set appropriate timeout (default 3s is too short for many tasks)
✅ Return proper error format (don't swallow exceptions)

SECURITY:
✅ IAM execution role with LEAST privilege (not AdministratorAccess)
✅ Never log sensitive data (PII, passwords, tokens) to CloudWatch
✅ Use Secrets Manager for secrets (not env vars — visible in console)
✅ Enable X-Ray tracing for debugging and performance insights
✅ Layer code: don't put secrets or credentials in Lambda layers

PERFORMANCE:
✅ Right-size memory (more memory = more CPU = often faster AND cheaper)
   Test: run at 256MB vs 512MB → if 512MB halves execution time → costs same!
✅ Avoid recursion (Lambda calling itself can create infinite loop)
✅ Use /tmp for temporary files (reused across invocations in warm env)
✅ Avoid loading large files on every invocation — use /tmp caching

ERROR HANDLING:
✅ Async invocations: configure DLQ or SNS on-failure destination
✅ SQS event source: configure partial batch response (don't fail whole batch)
✅ Wrap handler in try/catch, log error details, return appropriate response
✅ Use exponential backoff when calling external services

MONITORING:
✅ Structured logging (JSON) → CloudWatch Logs Insights queries easily
✅ Custom metrics: publish business metrics to CloudWatch
✅ X-Ray tracing: see exactly where time is spent in function
✅ Alarm: error rate, duration p99, throttles, concurrent executions
```

---

PERFORMANCE:

   Test: run at 256MB vs 512MB → if 512MB halves execution time → costs same!
✅ Avoid recursion (Lambda calling itself can create infinite loop)

✅ Avoid loading large files on every invocation — use /tmp caching

ERROR HANDLING:

✅ Initialize SDK clients and DB connections OUTSIDE handler
✅ Use environment variables for config (not hardcoded)
✅ Retrieve secrets from Secrets Manager (cache in memory between invocations)

✅ Return proper error format (don't swallow exceptions)

sections.title: 🎯 Interview Q&A — Lambda
sections.rawMarkdown:
**Q: Our Lambda suddenly started timing out and throttling during a production spike. How would you investigate?**
```
"I would treat that as a concurrency and downstream dependency incident, not just a Lambda incident.

First I would check four metrics together in CloudWatch:
→ ConcurrentExecutions
→ Throttles
→ Duration p95/p99
→ Errors

If concurrency spiked and throttles increased, I would verify:
→ account concurrency limit
→ reserved concurrency on that function
→ whether another noisy function consumed regional concurrency

Then I would check what changed in duration.
If duration jumped from 300ms to 8 seconds, the real issue is often downstream:
→ RDS connection exhaustion
→ external API slowdown
→ DynamoDB throttling
→ VPC/NAT bottleneck

For immediate mitigation:
→ set reserved concurrency for the critical function
→ reduce traffic using API Gateway throttling if needed
→ enable or increase Provisioned Concurrency if cold starts are part of problem
→ if database-backed, move through RDS Proxy and verify connection reuse

For async sources like SQS, I would also verify queue age and DLQ growth.
The senior answer is: Lambda scaling fast is helpful, but if the dependency behind it
cannot scale, Lambda becomes the thing that amplifies the outage."
```

**Q: How do you make Lambda safe for retries and duplicate events in real life?**
```
"Assume duplicates will happen. Design for idempotency from day one.

Where duplicates come from:
→ SQS visibility timeout expiry
→ EventBridge retry
→ client retry on timeout
→ Lambda async retry after transient failure

My pattern is:
1. Generate or extract an idempotency key
   Example: orderId, paymentId, eventId
2. Store processing status in DynamoDB with conditional write
   'only process if key does not exist'
3. Make side effects happen after idempotency check
4. Return success for already-processed events instead of failing

For payments specifically:
→ never charge card before idempotency check
→ store request fingerprint + final outcome
→ retries should return previous success, not create second charge

This is one of those questions where experienced engineers talk about duplicate-safe
business logic, not just Lambda retries."
```

**Q: When does a team regret choosing Lambda, even though the design looked good at first?**
```
"Usually when the workload is long-lived, stateful, or operationally noisy.

Common regret cases:
→ function runtime keeps growing close to 15 minutes
→ too many cold-start-sensitive endpoints
→ heavy VPC/database usage creates connection pressure
→ local debugging becomes painful across many small functions
→ event choreography becomes hard to reason about without strong tracing

At that point I ask:
→ should this be an always-on service on ECS?
→ should this workflow be Step Functions instead of many ad-hoc retries?
→ should we consolidate small functions into bounded-context services?

Lambda is excellent, but senior judgment is knowing when serverless complexity
starts exceeding the operational benefit."
```

---

cannot scale, Lambda becomes the thing that amplifies the outage."

This is one of those questions where experienced engineers talk about duplicate-safe
business logic, not just Lambda retries."

starts exceeding the operational benefit."

"I would treat that as a concurrency and downstream dependency incident, not just a Lambda incident.

First I would check four metrics together in CloudWatch:

If duration jumped from 300ms to 8 seconds, the real issue is often downstream:

For async sources like SQS, I would also verify queue age and DLQ growth.
The senior answer is: Lambda scaling fast is helpful, but if the dependency behind it
cannot scale, Lambda becomes the thing that amplifies the outage."

qaItems.question: How do you make Lambda safe for retries and duplicate events in real life?
qaItems.answer:
"Assume duplicates will happen. Design for idempotency from day one.

Where duplicates come from:
→ SQS visibility timeout expiry
→ EventBridge retry
→ client retry on timeout
→ Lambda async retry after transient failure

My pattern is:
1. Generate or extract an idempotency key
   Example: orderId, paymentId, eventId
2. Store processing status in DynamoDB with conditional write
   'only process if key does not exist'
3. Make side effects happen after idempotency check
4. Return success for already-processed events instead of failing

For payments specifically:
→ never charge card before idempotency check
→ store request fingerprint + final outcome
→ retries should return previous success, not create second charge

This is one of those questions where experienced engineers talk about duplicate-safe
business logic, not just Lambda retries."

qaItems.question: When does a team regret choosing Lambda, even though the design looked good at first?
qaItems.answer:
"Usually when the workload is long-lived, stateful, or operationally noisy.

Common regret cases:
→ function runtime keeps growing close to 15 minutes
→ too many cold-start-sensitive endpoints
→ heavy VPC/database usage creates connection pressure
→ local debugging becomes painful across many small functions
→ event choreography becomes hard to reason about without strong tracing

At that point I ask:
→ should this be an always-on service on ECS?
→ should this workflow be Step Functions instead of many ad-hoc retries?
→ should we consolidate small functions into bounded-context services?

Lambda is excellent, but senior judgment is knowing when serverless complexity
starts exceeding the operational benefit."

revisionPoints: → ConcurrentExecutions
→ Throttles
→ Duration p95/p99
→ Errors
→ account concurrency limit
→ reserved concurrency on that function
→ whether another noisy function consumed regional concurrency
→ RDS connection exhaustion

sections.title: 🔗 Connects To
sections.rawMarkdown:
```
Lambda ← API Gateway/ALB/Function URLs: synchronous HTTP triggers
Lambda ← S3/SNS/EventBridge: asynchronous events
Lambda ← SQS/Kinesis/DynamoDB Streams: polled event sources
Lambda → DynamoDB/S3/SQS/SNS: common managed dependencies
Lambda → RDS Proxy: safe relational DB access at scale
Lambda → CloudWatch/X-Ray: logs, metrics, traces
Lambda → IAM: execution role defines what code can access
Lambda → VPC: private resource access when needed

NEXT: Containers — ECS, ECR, EKS
When the workload should keep running continuously instead of per-event execution.
```

---
